name: Bhatta Kallata
dates_notes: Direct disciple of Vasugupta; flourished in Kashmir under King Avantivarman (855–883 CE), per Kalhaṇa's Rājataraṅgiṇī 5.66 (court-record-grade dating).
core_thesis: Bhaṭṭa Kallaṭa is the direct disciple of Vasugupta and the figure who, on one important strand of Trika tradition, is the actual author of the *Spanda-Kārikā* — the verse-text that the other strand credits to Vasugupta himself. (The internal-Trika dispute about the *Kārikā*'s authorship is genuine, runs into the school's earliest commentarial layer, and cannot be settled on present evidence.) On either ascription, Kallaṭa is the figure who brings the *Spanda* doctrine into systematic form: in his prose *Spanda-Sarva-Sva* ("the Whole of Spanda") he gives the school's first sustained articulation of *spanda* as the pulsation of consciousness by which manifestation as a whole occurs (*Spanda-Sarva-Sva* on *Spanda-Kārikā* 1.1, *yasyonmeṣa-nimeṣābhyāṃ jagataḥ pralayodayau*). *Spanda* is thus the dynamism of *manifestation as a whole* — closer in scope to *prakṛti* itself or to *mahāmāyā* than to any single *guṇa*, and definitively not equatable with *rajas* (the totality-scope is established by the *jagataḥ* of *SpK* 1.1 covering the entire cosmic field). Where Utpaladeva's later *Pratyabhijñā* programme will analyse consciousness through the *prakāśa–vimarśa* structure (*ĪPK* 1.5.10–17), Kallaṭa's *Spanda* analyses it through the *unmeṣa–nimeṣa* (unfolding–folding) rhythm that is the school's distinctive contribution (*SpK* 1.1; *Spanda-Sarva-Sva* ad loc.).

engaged_works.title: Spanda-Karika
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Authorship disputed within the school's own tradition: one tradition credits the Kārikā to Bhaṭṭa Kallaṭa as a systematic verse-distillation of the Spanda doctrine implicit in his teacher Vasugupta's Śiva-Sūtra; the other tradition (followed by Kṣemarāja in the Spanda-Nirṇaya) credits the Kārikā to Vasugupta himself. Both ascriptions have internal Trika support and cannot be settled on present evidence; the entry honours the dispute by listing the work under both authors with parallel notes.

ascription_evidence.description: Two competing traditions of ascription, both with internal Trika support.
Both ascriptions are accepted as legitimate within different sub-strands of the Trika tradition.

engaged_works.summary: The fifty-three verses of the foundational text of the *Spanda* tradition, organised into four *niṣyandas*. The first (*Spanda-Kārikā* 1.1–25) establishes the doctrine of *spanda*: consciousness is intrinsically a pulsation (*spanda*) by which the entire manifest universe is continuously projected, sustained, and reabsorbed (*SpK* 1.1, *yasyonmeṣa-nimeṣābhyāṃ jagataḥ pralayodayau*). *Spanda* is the dynamism of *manifestation as a whole* — closer in scope to *prakṛti* itself or to *mahāmāyā* than to any single *guṇa*, and definitively not equatable with *rajas* (the totality-scope is established by the *jagataḥ* of *SpK* 1.1). The second *niṣyanda* (*SpK* 2.1–7) analyses the operation of *spanda* in the bound subject; the third (*SpK* 3.1–19) gives the practical *upāyas* by which awareness aligns with the *spanda*; the fourth (*SpK* 4.1–2) gives the condition of full recognition. On the tradition that Kallaṭa is the author, the *Spanda-Kārikā* is the systematic distillation of his teacher Vasugupta's revelatory *Śiva-Sūtra* into verse (*SpK* 1.1 paralleling *ŚS* 1.1 *caitanyam ātmā*); on the alternative tradition the *Kārikā* is itself Vasugupta's. The entry conforms to schema by listing the work under both potential authors with full ascription disclosure.

engaged_works.title: Spanda-Sarva-Sva
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored prose commentary on the Spanda-Kārikā; the school's first sustained prose articulation of the Spanda doctrine, irrespective of which way the Spanda-Kārikā authorship question is resolved.

ascription_evidence.description: Modern academic consensus (Dyczkowski editions and studies).

engaged_works.summary: Bhaṭṭa Kallaṭa's prose commentary on the *Spanda-Kārikā* and the school's first sustained articulation of the *Spanda* doctrine in expository form. The *Spanda-Sarva-Sva* — "the Whole of Spanda" — works through each *niṣyanda* of the *Kārikā* with an emphasis on the systematic exposition of *spanda* as the dynamism of *manifestation as a whole*: the *unmeṣa–nimeṣa* (unfolding–folding) rhythm by which consciousness continuously projects, sustains, and reabsorbs the entire experiential field (*Spanda-Sarva-Sva* on *SpK* 1.1, the foundational gloss). Kallaṭa is careful to distinguish *spanda* from any *guṇa*: *spanda* is closer in scope to *prakṛti* itself or to *mahāmāyā*, the engine of form rather than one of its modes (*SSv* on *SpK* 1.1, ad *jagataḥ pralayodayau*, on the totality-scope of the *spanda*-pulse). The work also gives the school's earliest articulation of the soteriological program of attentive *vimarśa* on the *spanda*-rhythm (*SSv* on *SpK* 2.1–7), which Kṣemarāja will later integrate with the Pratyabhijñā analysis (*Spanda-Nirṇaya* prefatory *bhūmikā*). The Sanskrit prose register is austere and analytic — pre-dating the more ornate commentarial style that Abhinavagupta and Kṣemarāja will later develop — and the work is consequently a key source for reconstructing what the *Spanda* tradition looked like *before* it was drawn into the broader Trika curriculum of the Tantrāloka.

reception_notes: Bhaṭṭa Kallaṭa is the figure who brings the Spanda doctrine into systematic form within the Trika lineage. Kṣemarāja's Spanda-Nirṇaya and Spanda-Sandoha, written in the eleventh century, build directly on Kallaṭa's earlier Spanda-Sarva-Sva, although Kṣemarāja accepts the alternative ascription of the Kārikā to Vasugupta. Mark Dyczkowski's studies are the principal modern treatment of the Spanda tradition and of Kallaṭa's place within it.
